---
title: "Finance with SigAlg I: Introduction and binomial pricing models"
toc: true
date: "2026-04-09"
jupyter: site
categories: [Finance, Pricing models, Binomial pricing models, Probability theory, Measure theory, Stochastic processes, Martingales, SigAlg, Python]
draft: true
bibliography: /Users/johnmyers/dev/site/aux-files/references.bib
csl: /Users/johnmyers/dev/site/aux-files/american-mathematical-society-numeric.csl
# image: "sigalg-logo.png"
open-graph:
    title: "Finance with SigAlg I: Introduction and binomial pricing models"
    # image: "https://johnmyers-phd.com/posts/torch-der/torch-der-logo.png"
---

::: {.content-hidden}
$$
{{< include /aux-files/custom.tex >}}
$$
:::

```{python}
# | echo: false

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches

plt.style.use("../../aux-files/custom-theme.mplstyle")
yellow = "#FFC300"
```

One of the core problems in mathematical finance is the construction of models of prices of financial assets, whether these be commodity assets like agricultural goods or precious metals, equity assets like stocks, or practically anything traded on a market. Price models serve multiple purposes, including forecasting future prices, allowing investors to (attempt to) turn a profit through speculation, or allowing a firm to hedge its position against an undesirable movement in the price of a business-critical asset. They may also be used to price a contract written on the asset from which it *derives* its value—such contracts are known, naturally, as *derivative* contracts. (We will explore these contracts in the next post in this series.) From a scientific perspective, an accurate price model can help lend insight into some of the underlying economic mechanisms that drive prices.

Of course, the ultimate driver of an asset price is "the market" itself, by which we mean the relative levels of supply and demand of the asset. But the supply and demand curves are themselves shaped by innumerable forces, all connected and interacting in unimaginably complex ways. There is an analogy here with statistical physics: Though we might understand the individual microscopic mechanics of an isolated molecule of a gas, and how it would interact with another (single) molecule, when that molecule is included in a container of the gas, the number of inter-molecular interactions is so large that the state of the gas must be modeled probabilistically. Indeed, one might, in principle, determine the exact future state of every molecule of the gas, but the lesson taught to us by statistical physics is that it is more advantageous to treat the movements of the individual molecules as *random*, and then familiar macroscopic properties of the gas (like temperature) emerge as statistical measures derived from the underlying probability distributions.

Thus, through analogy, we might imagine that if we had access to complete information on the forces that shape the supply and demand curves, then we could predict with exact precision the price movements of all the assets traded on a financial market. But this is obviously not possible, and so we resort to modeling the price movements as random, just as a physicist does with the molecules of their gas. To complete the analogy, "macroscopic properties" of the assets and the contracts written on them (like *risk-neutral prices*) emerge as statistical measures derived from the underlying probability distributions.

As with mathematical modeling of any sort, there is a trade-off between real-world accuracy and parsimony: Usually, the more accurate a model, the more complex it is, perhaps with many more parameters than a simpler but less accurate model. The two types of price models discussed in this post—*binomial* and *trinomial pricing models*—have extremely simple structure, depending on only a handful of parameters. But this doesn't mean that they are not useful. In fact, as expert readers know, many of the intricate structures used in more complex price models are present in these basic models in simplified form, so a prior study of these models is beneficial.

This post introduces these two models through the new `finance` module of the [SigAlg](https://johnmyers-phd.com/sigalg/) Python package for measure-theoretic probability. SigAlg is designed to be an interface between abstract mathematics and concrete code. Mathematical objects that one manipulates with pen and paper—probability measures, $\sigma$-algebras, expectations and the like—all have representations in SigAlg, which differentiates it from purely computational Python libraries in which the underlying mathematical structures are left implicit. SigAlg directly exposes these structures.

### Prerequisites

On the coding side, the prerequisites are minimal: The SigAlg API is written with fidelity to the mathematics as a core design principle, so as long as the reader can understand very basic Python and they have a good command of the mathematics, they should have little trouble following along with the code in this post.

On the mathematical side, the prerequisites are a bit steeper: We will freely use the language of basic measure-theoretic probability theory and stochastic process theory. We expect the reader to be familiar with probability measures, (filtered) $\sigma$-algebras, (conditional) expectations, Radon-Nikodym derivatives, martingales, *etc*.

## The binomial model

The *binomial price models* in this post are examples of *geometric processes*, by which we mean the following. Suppose that $S_t$ is a discrete-state stochastic process, with indices drawn from the discrete set $t\in \{0,1,\ldots,T\}$, where $T$ is the *time horizon*. We imagine that $S_t$ is the price of an asset at time $t$, with $S_0$ being the initial (constant) price. Then $S_t$ is a *geometric process* if there is a second process $Z_t$ such that, for each time $t\geq 1$, we have

$$
S_t = S_{t-1}Z_t.
$$ {#eq-geo-model}

The stochastic process $Z_t$ is called the *driving process* and it is required to be an IID process, which means that the random variables $Z_1,Z_2,\ldots,Z_T$ are independent and identically distributed. If we remember that a sequence of real numbers $s_0,s_1,\ldots,s_T$ is called a *geometric sequence* if there is a constant $z$ such that

$$
s_t = s_{t-1}z
$$ {#eq-geo-seq}

for each $t\geq 1$, then the reason $S_t$ is called a *geometric* process is clear upon comparison of ([-@eq-geo-model]) and ([-@eq-geo-seq]). (The random variable $Z_t$ is "constant" in the sense that it is drawn from an IID sequence.) Both the binomial models described in this post, and the trinomial models described in the next, are of the form ([-@eq-geo-model])—they only differ in the identity of their driving processes.

A geometric process may be contrasted with an *arithmetic process*, in which ([-@eq-geo-model]) is replaced with the additive relation

$$
S_t = S_{t-1} + Z_t,
$$ {#eq-arith-model}

where $Z_t$ is again an IID driving process. Of course, arithmetic processes are more commonly known as *random walks*.

Through a log transform, a geometric process satisfying ([-@eq-geo-model]) may be converted into an arithmetic process:

$$
\log{S_t} = \log{S_{t-1}} + \log{Z_t}.
$$

Thus, if asset prices follow a geometric process, then the log prices are a random walk. This has financial implications, because analysts are often interested in the per-period *return* $(S_t-S_{t-1})/S_{t-1}$ of an asset price. Using the usual tangent line approximation, we have

$$
\frac{S_t -S_{t-1}}{S_{t-1}} \approx \log{(S_t/S_{t-1})} = \log{S_t} - \log{S_{t-1}},
$$

and so returns are (approximately) the (backward) difference of a random walk process. But such differences are just IID processes:

$$
\log{S_t} - \log{S_{t-1}} = \log{Z_t}.
$$

Hence, if asset prices are modeled as a geometric process, then returns are modeled as an IID process.

Now, moving on to the central definition of this post:

::: {#def-binomial}
Let $S_t$ be a geometric process, with driving process $Z_t$, and let $u$ and $d$ be two positive real numbers with $d < u$. Then $S_t$ is called a *binomial model* with *up-factor* $u$ and *down-factor* $d$ if the range of each $Z_t$ is $\{u,d\}$.
:::

Besides the initial price $S_0$ and the time horizon $T$, a binomial model is parametrized by its up- and down-factors $u$ and $d$. But there's a fifth parameter hidden in the driving process: Indeed, by the IID assumption, there is a parameter $p$, with $0 < p < 1$, such that $Z_t = u$ with probability $p$, and $Z_t = d$ with probability $1-p$ (for all $t$). In the context of pricing, the probability $p$ is often called the *real-world probability*.

An example of a price trajectory (i.e., sample path) of a binomial model would be a sequence of prices of the form

$$
S_0 \xrightarrow{u} S_0u \xrightarrow{d} S_0 ud \xrightarrow{u} S_0u^2d.
$$

Thus, the complete set of *all* price trajectories of a binomial model with time horizon $T$ is in one-to-one correspondence with the set of all $T$-tuples drawn from the set $\{u,d\}$, and hence there are $2^T$ possible price trajectories. These price trajectories are often visualized as paths through a (sideways) binary tree. For example, suppose that we consider a binomial model with the parameters

$$
S_0 = 100, \quad u = 1.3, \quad d = 0.9, \quad T=3.
$$

Then there are two possible prices at $t=1$, either

$$
S_0u = 130.00 \quad \text{or} \quad S_0d = 90.00.
$$

Similarly, there are three possible prices at $t=2$, either

$$
S_0u^2 = 169.00, \quad S_0ud = 117.00, \quad \text{or} \quad S_0d^2 = 81.00.
$$

All $2^3=8$ possible price trajectories may be displayed as paths in the following tree, with an upward sloping edge corresponding to an up move (multiplication by $u$), and a downward sloping edge corresponding to a down move (multiplication by $d$).

```{python}
# | fig-align: center
# | echo: false

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))

ax.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], color=yellow)
ax.plot([0, 1], [0, -1], color=yellow)

ax.plot([1, 2], [1, 2], color=yellow)
ax.plot([1, 2], [1, 0], color=yellow)

ax.plot([1, 2], [-1, 0], color=yellow)
ax.plot([1, 2], [-1, -2], color=yellow)

ax.plot([2, 3], [2, 3], color=yellow)
ax.plot([2, 3], [2, 1], color=yellow)

ax.plot([2, 3], [0, 1], color=yellow)
ax.plot([2, 3], [0, -1], color=yellow)

ax.plot([2, 3], [-2, -1], color=yellow)
ax.plot([2, 3], [-2, -3], color=yellow)

ax.text(
    0,
    0,
    "100.00",
    ha="right",
    va="center",
    fontsize=12,
    zorder=4,
    bbox=dict(
        boxstyle="round,pad=0.3",
        facecolor="#121212",
        edgecolor="#FAF9F6",
        linewidth=1,
    ),
)

ax.text(
    1,
    1,
    "130.00",
    ha="center",
    va="center",
    fontsize=12,
    zorder=4,
    bbox=dict(
        boxstyle="round,pad=0.3",
        facecolor="#121212",
        edgecolor="#FAF9F6",
        linewidth=1,
    ),
)

ax.text(
    1,
    -1,
    "90.00",
    ha="center",
    va="center",
    fontsize=12,
    zorder=4,
    bbox=dict(
        boxstyle="round,pad=0.3",
        facecolor="#121212",
        edgecolor="#FAF9F6",
        linewidth=1,
    ),
)

ax.text(
    2,
    2,
    "169.00",
    ha="center",
    va="center",
    fontsize=12,
    zorder=4,
    bbox=dict(
        boxstyle="round,pad=0.3",
        facecolor="#121212",
        edgecolor="#FAF9F6",
        linewidth=1,
    ),
)

ax.text(
    2,
    0,
    "117.00",
    ha="center",
    va="center",
    fontsize=12,
    zorder=4,
    bbox=dict(
        boxstyle="round,pad=0.3",
        facecolor="#121212",
        edgecolor="#FAF9F6",
        linewidth=1,
    ),
)

ax.text(
    2,
    -2,
    "81.00",
    ha="center",
    va="center",
    fontsize=12,
    zorder=4,
    bbox=dict(
        boxstyle="round,pad=0.3",
        facecolor="#121212",
        edgecolor="#FAF9F6",
        linewidth=1,
    ),
)

ax.text(
    3,
    3,
    r"$219.70 = S_0 u^3$",
    ha="left",
    va="center",
    fontsize=12,
    zorder=4,
    bbox=dict(
        boxstyle="round,pad=0.3",
        facecolor="#121212",
        edgecolor="#FAF9F6",
        linewidth=1,
    ),
)

ax.text(
    3,
    1,
    r"$152.10 = S_0 u^2d$",
    ha="left",
    va="center",
    fontsize=12,
    zorder=4,
    bbox=dict(
        boxstyle="round,pad=0.3",
        facecolor="#121212",
        edgecolor="#FAF9F6",
        linewidth=1,
    ),
)

ax.text(
    3,
    -1,
    r"$105.30 = S_0 ud^2$",
    ha="left",
    va="center",
    fontsize=12,
    zorder=4,
    bbox=dict(
        boxstyle="round,pad=0.3",
        facecolor="#121212",
        edgecolor="#FAF9F6",
        linewidth=1,
    ),
)

ax.text(
    3,
    -3,
    r"$72.90 = S_0 d^3$",
    ha="left",
    va="center",
    fontsize=12,
    zorder=4,
    bbox=dict(
        boxstyle="round,pad=0.3",
        facecolor="#121212",
        edgecolor="#FAF9F6",
        linewidth=1,
    ),
)

ax.text(
    0,
    4,
    r"$t=0$",
    ha="center",
    va="center",
    fontsize=12,
)

ax.text(
    1,
    4,
    r"$t=1$",
    ha="center",
    va="center",
    fontsize=12,
)

ax.text(
    2,
    4,
    r"$t=2$",
    ha="center",
    va="center",
    fontsize=12,
)

ax.text(
    3,
    4,
    r"$t=3$",
    ha="left",
    va="center",
    fontsize=12,
)

fig.suptitle("Price process 'S'")
ax.axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The mapping from price trajectories to terminal prices is many-to-one. For example, there are three trajectories leading to the final price $152.10$, namely, those corresponding to the following sequences of up and down moves:

$$
(u, u, d), \quad (u, d, u), \quad \text{and} \quad (d, u, u).
$$

Turning to SigAlg, a binomial model is represented as an instance of the `BinomialPricingModel` class. Such an object is built using the parameters identified above, along with a parameter called `risk_free_rate`. We shall have much more to say about this latter parameter below, but for now, we set it equal to some value and then ignore it.

```{python}

from sigalg.core import Time
from sigalg.finance import BinomialPricingModel

S_0 = 100  # initial price
r = 0.05  # risk-free rate — ignore for now
p = 0.7  # real-world probability
u = 1.3  # up-factor
d = 0.9  # down-factor
T = 3  # time horizon

# Instantiate a time index
time = Time.discrete(length=T)

# Instantiate a binomial model
S = BinomialPricingModel(
    initial_price=S_0,
    risk_free_rate=r,
    up_prob=p,
    up_factor=u,
    down_factor=d,
    time=time,
    name="S",
)
```

The class `BinomialPricingModel` is a subclass of SigAlg's `StochasticProcess`, and as such, each binomial pricing model carries two methods to generate price trajectories, namely `from_enumeration` and `from_simulation`. The latter method generates Monte Carlo simulations of price trajectories, while the former exhaustively enumerates all trajectories. We invoke this first method, and use SigAlg's interface with Matplotlib to visualize the trajectories:

```{python}
# | fig-align: center

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

_, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 3))
yellow = "#FFC300"

S.from_enumeration()
S.plot_trajectories(ax=ax, colors=[yellow], y_label="price")
plt.show()
```

Notice that this printout is essentially the same as the binary tree displayed earlier, with four terminal prices. We can also print the price process itself:

```{python}
print(S)
```

The printout shows the $2^3 = 8$ trajectories over the time period ending at $T=3$, all beginning at the initial price $S_0=100$. The trajectories are indexed by the set $\Omega = \{0,1,\ldots,7\}$, which serves as the domain of the process:

```{python}
print(S.domain)
```

The component random variables themselves $S_t: \Omega \to \mathbb{R}$ may be accessed via subscripting, which returns an instance of the `RandomVariable` class:

```{python}
print(S[2], "\n")  # Print the random variable S_2
print(S[2].domain, "\n")  # Check its domain
print(type(S[2]))  # Check its type
```

The cardinality of the sample space $\Omega$ is $2^T$, which scales exponentially with the time horizon. Obviously, this means that exhaustive enumeration of price trajectories is infeasible for large $T$. SigAlg provides two options that the user can leverage for longer time horizons. The first is to operate the `from_enumeration` method in `sparse` enumeration mode. This requires that the down-factor $d$ is equal to the reciprocal up-factor, $d = 1/u$, so that the binary tree is *recombining*. We make this adjustment in the next code block, and plot the sparsely enumerated trajectories over the same time horizon $T=3$ as before:

```{python}
# | fig-align: center

S.down_factor = 1 / u  # Reset the down-factor
S.from_enumeration(enum_mode="sparse")
print(S)

_, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 3))
S.plot_trajectories(ax=ax, colors=[yellow], y_label="price")
plt.show()
```

In `sparse` enumeration mode, the only price trajectories that are generated are those that consist first of a sequence entirely of down moves, followed by a sequence entirely of up moves. Every node/price in the binary tree is visited by one of these trajectories, and there are only $T+1$ of them, allowing one to generate all prices for large $T$. One of the downsides—as we shall see in a later post—is that we cannot use a binomial model in `sparse` enumeration mode to price path-dependent contingent claims.

The other option available to the user for longer time horizons is to use the `from_simulation` method mentioned above, which randomly samples price trajectories according to the real-world probability measure (see the next section). The following code block gives an example of its usage over a (slightly) longer time horizon $T=10$:

```{python}
# | fig-align: center

T = 10
S.time = Time.discrete(length=T)  # Set a longer time horizon
S.down_factor = d  # Reset to the original down-factor

S.from_simulation(n_trajectories=5, random_state=42)

_, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 3))
S.plot_trajectories(ax=ax, colors=[yellow], y_label="price")
plt.show()
```

## $\sigma$-algebras and filtrations

Every stochastic process $S_t$ is defined on an ambient probability space $(\Omega, \mathcal{F},P)$, where $\mathcal{F}$ is a $\sigma$-algebra of events in the sample space $\Omega$ and $P$ is a probability measure defined on $\mathcal{F}$. To be clear, this means that each function $S_t:\Omega \to \mathbb{R}$ is a random variable in the technical sense, i.e., each is $\mathcal{F}$-measurable.

For the binomial models $S_t$ considered in this post, SigAlg automatically constructs the underlying probability space $(\Omega,\mathcal{F},P)$. The sample space $\Omega$ was described above: It consists simply of a finite index set $\Omega = \{0,1,\ldots,n\}$ up to some non-negative integer $n$. As an example, the sample space for our first binomial model from the previous section is displayed in the first column of the following printout:

```{python}
T = 3
S.time = Time.discrete(length=3)

S.from_enumeration()
print(S)
```

The sample space is $\Omega = \{0,1,\ldots,7\}$. The $\sigma$-algebra is the power set on $\Omega$:

```{python}
# | echo: false

S._data = S._data.round(2)
```

```{python}
print(S.sigma_algebra)
```

To understand the printout, we recall that every $\sigma$-algebra on a *finite* sample space $\Omega$ is determined by its *atoms*, which are the minimal non-empty events. The atoms $\{A_i\}_{i\in I}$ (indexed by some finite set $I$) partition the sample space
$$
\Omega = \bigcup_{i\in I} A_i.
$$

In SigAlg, $\sigma$-algebras are represented by mappings $\Omega \to I$ from sample points $\omega$ to *atom identifiers* $i$, where $i$ is the index of the unique atom $A_i$ that contains $\omega$. In the printout above, the set $I$ of atom identifers is evidently the set of trajectories themselves, and so the atoms of the default $\sigma$-algebra are all eight singletons. In other words, the $\sigma$-algebra is indeed the power set.

Notice in the printout that the name of the default $\sigma$-algebra is $\sigma(S)$. The reader familiar with measure theory will recognize that this notation refers to the $\sigma$-algebra *generated* by $S$. In this context, we view the stochastic process as a $4$-dimensional *random vector*:

$$
S: \Omega \to \mathbb{R}^4, \quad S(\omega) = (S_0(\omega),S_1(\omega), S_2(\omega), S_3(\omega)).
$$

Reflecting this view as a random vector, stochastic processes in SigAlg are actually callable; for example:

```{python}
print(S(2))
```

Note that this is precisely the trajectory $(S_0(2),S_1(2),S_2(2),S_3(2))$ corresponding to the sample point $2\in \Omega$.

Now, $S$ (as a random vector) generates a $\sigma$-algebra whose atoms are precisely its nonempty level sets:

$$
S^{-1}(s) = \{ \omega \in \Omega \mid S(\omega) = s \},
$$

for $s\in \mathbb{R}^4$. Notice that these level sets are indeed naturally indexed by $4$-tuples $s$, i.e., trajectories of the stochastic process.

In a similar way, each component random variable $S_t$ of the stochastic process (and indeed *every* random variable in SigAlg) induces its own $\sigma$-algebra, denoted $\sigma(S_t)$. As an example, in the following code block we print the atom identifiers of the $\sigma$-algebra generated by the terminal price $S_3$:

```{python}
print(S[3].sigma_algebra)
```

Notice again that the atom identifiers are the values $S_3(\omega)$ of $S_3$ itself, reflecting the fact that the atoms are the nonempty level sets:

$$
S_3^{-1}(s) = \{\omega \in \Omega \mid S_3(\omega) = s\},
$$

for $s\in \mathbb{R}$.

The power set $\sigma$-algebra $\sigma(S)$ shown above has the full trajectories of the stochastic process as its atom identifiers—but one can also use the "partial trajectories" as atom identifiers to construct $\sigma$-algebras. In fact, this leads to a *filtration* of the $\sigma$-algebra $\sigma(S)$, which is a nested sequence of the form

$$
\mathcal{F}_0 \subset \mathcal{F}_1 \subset \mathcal{F}_2 \subset \mathcal{F}_3 = \sigma(S),
$$

indexed over the same time horizon $T=3$. In this particular case, with the partial trajectories as atom identifiers, we obtain the so-called *natural filtration* of the stochastic process. It is accessible via the corresponding attribute:

```{python}
F = S.natural_filtration
print(F)
```

The printout shows the time index and the four $\sigma$-algebras $\mathcal{F}_t$ (for $t=0,1,2,3$). Notice that $\mathcal{F}_3$ coincides with $\sigma(S)$, and that $\mathcal{F}_0$ is the *trivial $\sigma$-algebra* whose only atom is the whole sample space $\Omega$ itself. Notice also that the atom identifiers of $\mathcal{F}_t$ are the length-$t$ partial trajectories. So, two sample points $\omega,\omega'\in\Omega$ are in the same atom of $\mathcal{F}_t$ if and only if the full trajectories that they correspond to agree at all times up to (and including) time $t$, i.e.,

$$
S_r(\omega) = S_r(\omega')
$$ {#eq-partial-traj}

for each $r\leq t$. In view of ([-@eq-partial-traj]), the $\sigma$-algebra $\mathcal{F}_t$ is often written as

$$
\mathcal{F}_t = \sigma( S_0, S_1, \ldots, S_t),
$$

and it is called the $\sigma$-algebra *generated* by the random variables $S_0,S_1,\ldots, S_t$.

The natural filtration is represented in SigAlg as an instance of the `Filtration` class. As such, it is subscriptable, allowing us to access the component $\sigma$-algebras:

```{python}
print(F[1])
```

Finally, note that the "information" encoded in the $\sigma$-algebra $\mathcal{F}_t$ only allows to distinguish between trajectories based on their paths up to time $t$. The $\sigma$-algebra $\mathcal{F}_t$ cannot "see into the future" beyond time $t$. At one end of the time interval, $\mathcal{F}_0$ has only one atom, and so it cannot be used to distinguish between *any* of the trajectories. At the other end, $\mathcal{F}_3$ corresponds to the power set $\sigma$-algebra, and so it contains enough information to distinguish between *all* of the trajectories.

## Probability measures

Having described the sample space $\Omega$ and the $\sigma$-algebra $\mathcal{F}$ underlying our binomial model, we now turn to the probability measure $P$. This will complete our description of the full probability space $(\Omega, \mathcal{F},P)$.

Recall that our model was instantiated with a real-world probability $p=0.7$, which was the probability that the driving process takes the value $Z_t=u$, where $u$ is the up-factor. This probability is accessible via the `up_prob` attribute:

```{python}
print(S.up_prob)
```

Using the real-world probability $p$, we define a discrete probability measure $P$ on the sample space by setting

$$
P(\{\omega\}) = p^k (1-p)^{T-k}
$$

for each $\omega\in \Omega$, provided that $\omega$ corresponds to a trajectory with $k$ up moves and $T-k$ down moves. (Recall $T$ is the time horizon.) This probability measure is accessible using the `probability_measure` attribute of the binomial model, which returns an instance of SigAlg's `ProbabilityMeasure` class:

```{python}
P = S.probability_measure
print(P, "\n")  # Print the measure
print(type(P))  # Check its type
```

For easy comparison, we can print the probabilities next to the trajectories:

```{python}
S.print_trajectories_and_probabilities()
```

Thus, we see that the probability of the event $A = \{0,7\}$ is

$$
P(A) = 0.7^3 +0.3^3  = 0.37.
$$

We may perform the same computation in SigAlg:

```{python}
A = S.domain.get_event([0, 7])  # A is an `Event` object
print(round(P(A), 3))
```

Probability measures in SigAlg may also be treated like probability mass functions and called directly on sample points:

```{python}
print(round(P(0) + P(7), 3))
```

When we extract component random variables, we inherit the probability measure. For example:

```{python}
print(S[3].probability_measure)
```

As we learned in our first course on probability theory, every random variable induces a *pushforward* (or *image*) *probability measure* on its range. In the case of the component random variable $S_3$, this is the discrete measure $P_{S_3}$ on $\mathbb{R}$ defined by

$$
P_{S_3}(\{s\}) = P\left( \{\omega \in \Omega \mid S_3(\omega)=s\}\right),
$$

for each $s\in \mathbb{R}$. The support of this measure is the range of $S_3$, namely, the set of final prices $\{72.90, 105.30, 152.10, 219.70\}$. To obtain the pushforward measure $P_{S_3}$ in SigAlg, we call the `pushforward` method:

```{python}
S[3].pushforward()
```

## $L^2$-spaces

The collection of all random variables on the probability space $(\Omega, \sigma(S), P)$ underlying our binomial model constitute a finite-dimensional real vector space denoted $L^2(\Omega,\sigma(S),P)$. Here, by a random variable "on the probability space," we mean that each random variable must be $\sigma(S)$-measurable.[If the reader recalls, $\sigma(S)$ is the power set $\sigma$-algebra, and so *every* function on $\Omega$ is $\sigma(S)$-measurable. But we will see examples below of $L^2$-spaces built from coarser $\sigma$-algebras, so we still prefer to explicitly state this condition.]{.aside} And, actually, in the general mathematical theory a function $X: \Omega \to \mathbb{R}$ must also be such that

$$
\int_\Omega X^2 \, dP < \infty
$$ {#eq-square-integrable}

in order to be in the $L^2$-space, in addition to measurability. However, all sample spaces in SigAlg are finite and probability measures are discrete, so the Lebesgue integral above turns into a finite sum:

$$
\int_\Omega X^2 \, dP = \sum_{\omega \in \Omega} X(\omega)^2P(\{\omega\}).
$$

But the right-hand side is always $<\infty$ in SigAlg, and hence this extra condition ([-@eq-square-integrable]) is superfluous.

As the experienced reader knows, elements in an $L^2$-space are often taken to be equivalence classes of random variables, where the equivalence relation is given by *almost sure equality*, meaning that two random variables $X$ and $Y$ are identified in the $L^2$-space if they are equal everywhere except on an event of probability $0$. However, in SigAlg, elements in an $L^2$-space are actual random variables. For an example, let's build the $L^2$-space

$$
H = L^2(\Omega, \sigma(S),P)
$$

using the probability space from our binomial pricing model:

```{python}

from sigalg.l2 import L2

H = L2(
    sample_space=S.domain,
    sigma_algebra=S.sigma_algebra,
    probability_measure=S.probability_measure,
)

# Check that S_3 is in H, for example
print(S[3] in H)
```

The Lebesgue integral

$$
\int_A X \, dP = \sum_{\omega \in A} X(\omega) P(\{\omega\})
$$

of a random variable $X\in H$ over an event $A\in \sigma(S)$ is computable in SigAlg through an instance method of the `L2` class. For example, in the next code block we print out the trajectories of our binomial model along with the probability measure, and then compute $\int_A S_3 \, dP$, where $A = \{0,3\}$:

```{python}

S.print_trajectories_and_probabilities()

A = S.domain.get_event([0, 3])
integral = H.integrate(rv=S[3], event=A)
print("The integral is", round(integral, 3))
```

The `event` parameter of the `integrate` method is optional; if it is omitted, then the event defaults to the entire sample space $A=\Omega$, and the Lebesgue integral is the expectation:

$$
E(X) = \int_\Omega X \, dP.
$$

In SigAlg:

```{python}
expectation = H.integrate(rv=S[3])
print("The expectation is", round(expectation, 3))
```

Alternatively, the expectation may be computed via the `expectation` method on the random variable itself; however, this method returns an instance of the `RandomVariable` class, and not a number:

```{python}
expectation_rv = S[3].expectation()
print(expectation_rv)
```

Note that this is the *constant* random variable whose unique value is equal to the expectation computed via the integral. The reason that this method yields a random variable is to make it consistent with *conditional* expectations $E(X\mid \mathcal{F})$ as random variables, where $\mathcal{F}$ is a $\sigma$-algebra contained in $\sigma(S)$; see below. The unconditional expectation $E(X)$ is then obtained as the unique value of the random variable $E(X \mid \{\emptyset, \Omega\})$, where the latter expectation is conditioned on the trivial $\sigma$-algebra.

The theoretical interest in $L^2$-spaces stems primarily from the rich mathematical structure that they carry—indeed, they are examples of *Hilbert spaces* when equipped with the inner product given by:

$$
\langle X, Y \rangle = \int_{\Omega} XY \, dP = \sum_{\omega \in \Omega} X(\omega)Y(\omega)P(\{\omega\}).
$$

This inner product is implemented in SigAlg as an instance method of the `L2` class:

```{python}
# Compute the inner product of the random variables S_1 and S_2
inner_product = H.inner(S[1], S[2])
print(round(inner_product, 3))
```

The induced norm of a random variable is defined by the equation

$$
\|X\|^2 = \langle X, X \rangle.
$$

This too is computable in SigAlg:

```{python}
# Compute the norm of the random variable S_1
norm = H.norm(S[1])
print(round(norm, 3))
```

A basis for the vector space $H$ is given by the set of indicator functions of the atoms of $\sigma(S)$. But recall that $\sigma(S)$ is the power set and $\Omega$ has cardinality $8$; therefore, the $L^2$-space has dimension $8$:

```{python}
print(H.dim)
```

An orthogonal basis of $H$ is given by the collection of indicator functions $I_A:\Omega \to \mathbb{R}$ of the atoms $A$ of the $\sigma$-algebra, where

$$
I_A(\omega) = \begin{cases}
1 & : \omega \in A, \\
0 & : \omega \notin A.
\end{cases}
$$

So, an orthonormal basis of $H$ is given by the normalized indicator functions:

$$
\frac{I_A}{\|I_A\|} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{P(A)}} I_A,
$$

where $A$ varies over all atoms of $\sigma(S)$.

The same types of orthnormal bases exist if we change the $\sigma$-algebra of the $L^2$-space. For example, let's recall the $\sigma$-algebra $\mathcal{F}_1$ from the previous section, with two atoms:

```{python}
print(F[1])
```

We define a second $L^2$-space

$$
G = L^2(\Omega, \mathcal{F}_1, P)
$$

using this $\sigma$-algebra:

```{python}

G = L2(
    sample_space=S.domain,
    sigma_algebra=F[1],
    probability_measure=S.probability_measure,
)
```

Since $\mathcal{F}_1$ has only two atoms, the orthonormal basis of $G$ described above consists of only two functions; they are accessible via the `basis` attribute, which returns a dictionary whose values are the normalized indicator functions:

```{python}
G_basis = list(G.basis.values())
for indicator in G_basis:
    print(indicator)
```

Note that the names of the random variables are the atom identifiers, i.e., the partial trajectories of length $1$.

Now, because $\mathcal{F}_1$ is a sub-$\sigma$-algebra of $\sigma(S)$, the vector space $G$ is a subspace of $H$. In particular, the orthonormal basis of $G$ identified above is an orthonormal set in $H$. Therefore, given a random variable $X\in H$, its orthogonal projection $\widehat{X}$ onto $G$ is given by the familiar generalized Fourier expansion:

$$
\widehat{X} = \langle X, \phi_1 \rangle \phi_1 + \langle X, \phi_2 \rangle \phi_2,
$$

where $\phi_1$ and $\phi_2$ are the basis elements of $G$. For example, in SigAlg we may use this *exact* formula to compute the orthogonal projection of $S_3$ onto $G$:

```{python}
S_3_hat = sum(H.inner(S[3], phi) * phi for phi in G_basis)
print(S_3_hat.with_name("S_3_hat"))
```

But the expert reader knows that orthogonal projections of random variables in $L^2$-spaces are *exactly* conditional expectations. In fact, the random variable $\widehat{S}_3$ that we just computed is precisely $E(S_3 \mid \mathcal{F}_1)$, the expectation of $S_3$ given $\mathcal{F}_1$. These conditional expectations are directly computable in SigAlg by passing a value to the `sigma_algebra` parameter of the `expectation` method from above:

```{python}
print(S[3].expectation(sigma_algebra=F[1]))
```

As we know from Hilbert space theory, the orthogonal projection $\hat{S}_3$ is the solution of an optimization problem:

$$
\widehat{S}_3 = \underset{Y \in G}{\operatorname{argmin}} \|Y - S_3\|^2.
$$

But a random variable is in $G$ if and only if it is $\mathcal{F}_1$-measurable, and the norm may be expressed in terms of an expectation—therefore, the conditional expectation $E(S_3 \mid \mathcal{F}_1)= \widehat{S}_3$ minimizes the mean squared error

$$
E\left[(Y - S_3)^2\right]
$$

over all $\mathcal{F}_1$-measurable random variables $Y$.


## Bibliography
